_comment: To update this file: edit any module/stack/use-case entry, bump last_updated, commit, push. The site rebuilds within 60s. Each entry is self-contained — no rendering changes needed for additions.

intro.title: AI coding — From Noob to Expert
intro.body: A gradual path from zero AI knowledge to expert-level mastery. Seven stages take you from 'never used AI' to 'running agents in production', each a small set of resources to build your intuition. At the bottom: ten real-life use cases that turn the theory into things you can ship next weekend to get the most out of these new technologies.

tracks.label: Level 1: Noob — Start here
tracks.blurb: Zero AI knowledge → your first useful prompt

tracks.label: Level 2: The engine
tracks.blurb: How LLMs actually work, without hype

tracks.label: Level 3: Use it well
tracks.blurb: Get reliable output, pick the right model

tracks.label: Level 4: Code with it
tracks.blurb: AI in your editor and terminal

modules.title: AI in 10 minutes — for someone who's never used it
modules.why: Strip away the hype. An LLM is a text predictor wrapped in a chat interface. It doesn't know your data, doesn't remember between sessions, and is sometimes wrong. Get this right and the rest is engineering.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Day 1 with Salesforce — don't memorize features. Build a mental model of what the platform is and isn't, then dig in.

resources.title: What is ChatGPT actually doing?
resources.author: Stephen Wolfram
resources.summary: Long but the clearest plain-English explanation. Skim — read sections 1-3 carefully, scroll the rest.

resources.title: But what is a GPT?
resources.author: 3Blue1Brown
resources.summary: Visual, intuitive, no math. Best 30 minutes you'll spend if you've never seen this stuff.

resources.title: Anthropic's intro hub
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Vendor's beginner page. Useful for vocabulary.

modules.title: Your first hour with Claude or ChatGPT
modules.why: Skip the API. Open claude.ai or chatgpt.com and run four exercises: summarize a doc, draft an email, translate code from one language to another, ask 'why is this approach wrong?' on a design idea. Notice where it shines and where it bluffs — that intuition is the whole game.
modules.sf_bridge: Like first day in a sandbox org. Poke around, break things, build a feel before reading the docs cover-to-cover.

resources.title: Claude.ai
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Anthropic's chat. Free tier exists. Best default for serious work.

resources.title: ChatGPT
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: OpenAI's chat. Compare side-by-side with Claude on the same prompts — feel for the differences.

resources.title: How I use LLMs
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: Honest tour of one expert's daily workflow. Steal his patterns.

modules.title: 1 · Mental model: what an LLM actually is
modules.why: Before agents and spec-driven dev, you need a correct intuition for the engine. Most confusion downstream comes from treating an LLM like a database or a deterministic function — it is neither.
modules.sf_bridge: Think of an LLM as a stateless Apex method with no access to your org, whose only inputs are the text you pass in. Every call starts fresh. All the cleverness in tools like Cursor or Claude Code is arranging what text gets passed in.

resources.title: Things we learned about LLMs in 2024
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: Best single-page catch-up on how LLMs actually work today. Opinionated, concrete, no hype.

resources.title: Intro to large language models
resources.author: Andrej Karpathy
resources.summary: The definitive beginner-but-serious explainer. Watch at 1.25x.

resources.title: Prompt engineering
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Vendor docs, but the clearest short guide to writing prompts that get consistent output.

modules.title: Tokens, embeddings & attention
modules.why: Tokens are the atomic unit of cost and context. Embeddings store meaning as numbers. Attention is how the model decides what matters. You need a feel for all three before reading anything else.
modules.sf_bridge: Tokens ≈ API call units, metered. Embeddings ≈ semantic indexes for fuzzy matching across records. Attention ≈ a SOQL WHERE clause the model writes itself, on the fly, over your prompt.

resources.title: The Illustrated Transformer
resources.author: Jay Alammar
resources.summary: The classic visual explainer. Skip the math, keep the intuition.

resources.title: Attention in transformers, visually
resources.author: 3Blue1Brown
resources.summary: Best video on what attention actually computes.

resources.title: Embeddings guide
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: The 'how to use them' side. Vectors → similarity → retrieval.

modules.title: The context window is your governor limit
modules.why: Everything in agentic coding — retrieval, MCP, compaction, subagents — exists because of this one constraint. Understand it and the rest reads like obvious engineering.
modules.sf_bridge: Context window ≈ total SOQL rows + heap per transaction. Finite, priced, and if you blow past it the work fails. Every abstraction on top is a strategy to stay under limit.

resources.title: Prompt caching
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Why agents reuse a system prompt 50× per session. Cost model maps 1:1 to platform cache.

resources.title: How long contexts fail
resources.author: Lance Martin
resources.summary: Why 'just give the model the whole codebase' doesn't work, and what does.

modules.title: Temperature, sampling & determinism
modules.why: Same prompt, different output. Knowing why — and how to dial it down — is how you write reliable agent loops.
modules.sf_bridge: Temperature ≈ deciding whether a formula returns the same value every call or accepts noise. Production agents usually want temperature near zero — like deterministic Apex.

resources.title: Messages API parameters
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Where temperature, top_p, and stop sequences actually live in the API.

resources.title: Temperature in LLMs
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: Practical notes on when to tweak it and when not to bother.

modules.title: Hallucinations & how to mitigate them
modules.why: Confident-sounding wrong answers are the #1 production risk. Three antidotes: retrieval, tools, evals.
modules.sf_bridge: Hallucination ≈ a Flow path that runs silently with the wrong filter — looks fine, gives the wrong answer. You catch it the same way: tests and observability.

resources.title: Reducing hallucinations
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Concrete prompting techniques + when to add retrieval.

resources.title: Guide to hallucinations
resources.author: Lakera
resources.summary: Vendor blog, but the taxonomy is well-organized.

modules.title: Cost & latency economics
modules.why: Every prompt is a billed transaction. Knowing what's expensive (long context, reasoning, image input, web search) is operational hygiene.
modules.sf_bridge: Same instinct as governor limits and DML row counts — design for the budget envelope, monitor the bill, fail fast when you exceed.

resources.title: Anthropic pricing
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: The current price card. Bookmark it.

resources.title: OpenAI pricing
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: Same, OpenAI side. Note: reasoning models bill output for 'thinking' tokens you don't see.

resources.title: LLM pricing trends
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: How prices crashed. The trend matters more than the snapshot.

modules.title: Prompt engineering fundamentals
modules.why: Prompts are now part of your codebase. Treat them like Apex — versioned, reviewed, tested. Sloppy prompts are the new sloppy SOQL.
modules.sf_bridge: Prompts ≈ stored procedures or formula fields. They look like text but they're code. Lint them, version them, regress-test them.

resources.title: Prompt engineering overview
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: The clearest short reference. Read it twice.

resources.title: Prompt engineering guide
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: The same ideas, OpenAI's framing. Useful for cross-vendor work.

resources.title: Prompt engineering
resources.author: Lilian Weng
resources.summary: Deeper, technique-by-technique reference.

modules.title: Picking a frontier model — Claude, GPT, Gemini
modules.why: Three first-tier vendors set the ceiling. As of 2026: Claude Sonnet 4.7, GPT-5 / GPT-5-Codex, Gemini 3 Pro. Each has a use case where it's clearly best.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Salesforce vs Microsoft Dynamics vs Oracle — three first-tier vendors, each with strengths. Pick by workload and ecosystem fit, not loyalty.

resources.title: Claude models overview
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Which Claude is which. Sonnet 4.7 is the default for coding.

resources.title: OpenAI models
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: GPT-5 family + reasoning o-series mapped out.

resources.title: Gemini models
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Gemini 3 Pro + Flash + Nano. Strongest on long context.

modules.title: Chain-of-thought & reasoning models
modules.why: Reasoning models (o3, DeepSeek-R1, Claude with extended thinking, Gemini 3 Deep Think) think before answering. Different cost, latency, and capability profile — know when to reach for one.
modules.sf_bridge: Reasoning ≈ a Flow with a hidden 'thinking' subflow before the visible answer. You pay for both, but quality jumps on hard problems.

resources.title: Learning to reason with LLMs
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: The o1 announcement. Sets the conceptual frame.

resources.title: Extended thinking
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How Claude exposes reasoning tokens. Practical.

resources.title: Understanding reasoning LLMs
resources.author: Sebastian Raschka
resources.summary: The technical taxonomy — RL, distillation, scaling test-time compute.

modules.title: Multimodal models
modules.why: Vision, audio, video are first-class. Coding agents read screenshots; design tools generate from sketches; meeting transcripts feed straight into specs.
modules.sf_bridge: Like adding rich-content fields to your data model — same pipeline, more surface area. The modal becomes part of the schema.

resources.title: Vision (Claude)
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to send images and what the model sees.

resources.title: Vision (OpenAI)
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: Same idea, OpenAI side. Worth comparing limits.

resources.title: Gemini multimodal
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Gemini handles audio and video natively — useful for meeting → spec workflows.

modules.title: Coding-specialized models
modules.why: Beyond general-purpose models, code-tuned variants (Qwen 2.5 Coder, DeepSeek-Coder V2, Codestral, GPT-5-Codex) are cheaper and often better at completion specifically.
modules.sf_bridge: Like vertical clouds (Health Cloud, Financial Services Cloud) — same engine, tuned for one job, smaller licensing footprint.

resources.title: Qwen 2.5 Coder
resources.author: Alibaba
resources.summary: Best open coding model family. 1.5B → 32B sizes.

resources.title: DeepSeek-Coder
resources.author: DeepSeek
resources.summary: The code-specialized lineage that started the open-coding wave.

resources.title: Codestral
resources.author: Mistral
resources.summary: Mistral's coding model. Solid latency for inline completion.

modules.title: Claude Code
modules.why: Anthropic's terminal-native agent. Strong for spec-driven work, deep codebases, headless CI use, MCP integration.
modules.sf_bridge: Like SFDX CLI — terminal-first, scriptable, fits an architect's workflow. Composable with everything else in your toolchain.

resources.title: Claude Code overview
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Start here. Install + first run.

resources.title: Best practices
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How the team that builds it actually uses it.

resources.title: Subagents & MCP
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to extend Claude Code beyond the basics.

modules.title: Cursor
modules.why: VS Code fork with a built-in agent (Composer). Best for incremental edits, inline completion, and IDE-native pairing.
modules.sf_bridge: Like VS Code with the Salesforce extensions installed — the editor you live in, just smarter. Lowest cognitive switch cost from existing IDE habits.

resources.title: Cursor docs
resources.author: Cursor
resources.summary: The full feature surface. Read the agent + context sections.

resources.title: Composer Agent
resources.author: Cursor
resources.summary: Cursor's multi-file agent mode. The headline feature.

modules.title: OpenAI Codex (CLI + Cloud)
modules.why: OpenAI's relaunched coding agent — GPT-5-Codex tuned model, ships as a CLI and a cloud-managed agent. The OpenAI counterpart to Claude Code.
modules.sf_bridge: Same loop pattern as Claude Code, different vendor. Pick by which model you want driving the loop and which ecosystem you're in.

resources.title: Codex (OpenAI)
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: The product page. Read first for positioning.

resources.title: Codex CLI on GitHub
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: Open-source CLI client. Install + first run.

resources.title: Codex docs
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: API + advanced usage. Headless / programmatic.

modules.title: Aider
modules.why: Open-source CLI agent that works with any model. Git-integrated, scriptable, model-agnostic. Worth knowing as your fallback when commercial tools are blocked.
modules.sf_bridge: Like an open-source SFDX plugin — community-driven, swap the engine, your data stays yours.

resources.title: Aider site
resources.author: Aider
resources.summary: Install + features. Strong README.

resources.title: Aider on GitHub
resources.author: Aider
resources.summary: Source. Worth reading the architecture if you want to understand a coding agent end-to-end.

modules.title: WebStorm + JetBrains Junie
modules.why: JetBrains' agent (Junie) embedded in WebStorm / IntelliJ / PyCharm. For teams already in the JetBrains family, the lowest-friction agent rollout.
modules.sf_bridge: Like switching IDE plugins — your shortcuts and refactors stay; the AI is layered on top, not replacing the editor you trust.

resources.title: Junie
resources.author: JetBrains
resources.summary: JetBrains' agent. Autonomous mode + IDE-native.

resources.title: JetBrains AI Assistant
resources.author: JetBrains
resources.summary: The broader AI suite — completion, chat, refactor.

resources.title: WebStorm AI features
resources.author: JetBrains
resources.summary: WebStorm-specific feature page. Concrete examples.

modules.title: Google Antigravity
modules.why: Google's agent-first IDE (launched Nov 2025). Agents run in browser, terminal, and editor concurrently. Free preview, supports Gemini 3 + Claude + GPT models.
modules.sf_bridge: Like a brand-new IDE built around agents from day one — not retrofitted. The first serious 'agent-native' editor from a frontier lab.

resources.title: Antigravity
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Product site. Download + first impressions.

resources.title: Antigravity docs
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Walkthrough of agent-first workflows.

resources.title: Google blog announcement
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: The launch context. Why Google built a new IDE.

modules.title: Windsurf (Codeium)
modules.why: Codeium's agentic IDE. Strong for 'Cascade' multi-file edits. Acquired by Cognition in 2025 — now part of the Devin family.
modules.sf_bridge: Like another VS Code fork — pick by which agent loop fits your brain. Cascade has a distinctive multi-file flow worth trying.

resources.title: Windsurf
resources.author: Codeium / Cognition
resources.summary: Product site. Free tier exists.

resources.title: Cascade docs
resources.author: Windsurf
resources.summary: The multi-file agent flow that's the headline feature.

modules.title: Agents — what the word actually means
modules.why: 'Agent' is overloaded. In practice it's a loop: model writes output → some output is a tool call → tool runs → result fed back → repeat until done. Everything else is UX.
modules.sf_bridge: An agent ≈ a Flow that loops, calls Apex actions (tools), and decides its own next step. The 'model' replaces the decision nodes you'd build declaratively.

resources.title: Building effective agents
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: The canonical explainer. Distinguishes workflows (deterministic) from agents (model drives the loop).

resources.title: What are AI agents really?
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: Cuts through the marketing. 'Tools in a loop' — that's the whole thing.

resources.title: LLM Powered Autonomous Agents
resources.author: Lilian Weng
resources.summary: Deeper, more technical. Skip on first pass, return for the architecture taxonomy.

modules.title: ReAct & the tool-use loop
modules.why: ReAct (Reason → Act → Observe) is the loop pattern under almost every coding agent. Understand it once and Cursor / Claude Code / Codex / Aider all become readable.
modules.sf_bridge: ReAct ≈ a Flow with a Decision element followed by an Action element, in a loop, with each action's result fed back to the decision.

resources.title: ReAct paper
resources.author: Yao et al.
resources.summary: The original. Read sections 1-3.

resources.title: ReAct pattern in Python
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: A 100-line implementation. The whole loop fits on one screen.

resources.title: Agents (LangChain concepts)
resources.author: LangChain
resources.summary: Standard library framing of the same idea.

modules.title: Planning & decomposition
modules.why: For non-trivial work, the agent first writes a plan, then executes it. This single technique pushes success rates from 40% to 80% on multi-step tasks.
modules.sf_bridge: Like writing the technical design doc before the build. Architects already do this — agents are catching up.

resources.title: Claude Code best practices
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to structure prompts so the agent plans before it touches code.

resources.title: Planning matters
resources.author: Sean Goedecke
resources.summary: Why planning is the lever, not the model.

modules.title: Subagents & orchestration
modules.why: A parent agent spawns subagents with their own context window for subtasks. This is how long, multi-file refactors stay coherent.
modules.sf_bridge: Subagent ≈ Queueable Apex from a parent transaction — fresh limits, fresh context, returns a result to the parent.

resources.title: Multi-agent research system
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How Anthropic actually builds multi-agent products. Concrete patterns.

resources.title: Subagents in Claude Code
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How they're configured and when they help.

modules.title: Function calling fundamentals
modules.why: How models actually invoke external code. Same idea everywhere, slightly different schemas per vendor.
modules.sf_bridge: Function call ≈ Invocable Method called from a Flow. Typed inputs, typed outputs, side effects allowed.

resources.title: Tool use overview
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: The clearest explanation of the request/response shape.

resources.title: Function calling
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: Same idea, OpenAI's framing. Useful for cross-vendor work.

modules.title: MCP — the protocol
modules.why: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the emerging standard for plugging tools into any agent. Vendor-neutral. Adopted by Claude, OpenAI, Google in 2025.
modules.sf_bridge: MCP ≈ Named Credentials + External Services. Register a capability once, callable from any consumer with typed inputs.

resources.title: Introducing MCP
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: The announcement. Read first for the 'why'.

resources.title: MCP spec
resources.author: MCP
resources.summary: The protocol itself. Skim the architecture page; transport details can wait.

resources.title: MCP servers list
resources.author: MCP
resources.summary: Reference implementations + community-built servers. Worth a Saturday.

modules.title: Building your own MCP server
modules.why: To expose your own systems (Salesforce org, internal API, database) to any agent. The 2025 equivalent of writing an integration.
modules.sf_bridge: Like building a Connected App / External Service. Define the schema once, every agent can consume it without per-vendor work.

resources.title: MCP Python SDK
resources.author: MCP
resources.summary: Official Python SDK. Hello-world to working server in 30 minutes.

resources.title: MCP TypeScript SDK
resources.author: MCP
resources.summary: Same, TypeScript. Pick by your stack.

resources.title: Build an MCP server
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Anthropic's how-to with concrete code.

modules.title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
modules.why: Fetch relevant docs at query time, include them in the prompt. The pattern behind every 'chat with your data' product.
modules.sf_bridge: RAG ≈ SOQL + formula — pull the right records first, then reason over them. Without retrieval, the model is guessing from training data.

resources.title: RAG guide
resources.author: Pinecone
resources.summary: Vendor-flavored but the clearest beginner explainer.

resources.title: Contextual retrieval
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to make RAG actually work — embed chunks with surrounding context.

resources.title: RAG is dead, long live RAG
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.summary: Why most RAG implementations fail and how to evaluate yours.

modules.title: Spec-driven development
modules.why: The hottest paradigm of 2025. Instead of prompting your way to code, you write a precise spec; the agent implements + tests + iterates.
modules.sf_bridge: You already do spec-driven dev — it's called the solution design document before a build. The new part: the agent reads the spec and produces metadata + Apex.

resources.title: Spec-driven development with AI
resources.author: GitHub
resources.summary: Introduces GitHub's open-source Spec Kit. Clearest practical walkthrough.

resources.title: Spec Kit
resources.author: GitHub
resources.summary: The toolkit itself. Read the README, clone it, run the example.

resources.title: Specifications: the missing link
resources.author: Sean Goedecke
resources.summary: Why specs beat prompts for non-trivial work. Short, opinionated, right.

modules.title: Writing specs that work
modules.why: Bad specs produce bad code, regardless of model. Examples, contracts, constraints, and counter-examples are the levers.
modules.sf_bridge: Same discipline as a well-written user story with acceptance criteria. The story you'd hand a senior dev now goes to an agent — same quality bar.

resources.title: Long-context tips
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to structure 50-page specs so the model doesn't lose the plot.

resources.title: Every — Chain of Thought
resources.author: Various
resources.summary: Best ongoing newsletter on writing specs and managing AI workflows.

modules.title: Test generation from specs
modules.why: Best practice: agent writes the test first from the spec, then the implementation. TDD with infinite patience.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Apex test classes generated from acceptance criteria — but the agent writes both, then runs them, then iterates until they pass.

resources.title: Aider tutorials
resources.author: Aider
resources.summary: Concrete TDD workflows with a coding agent.

resources.title: Claude Code best practices
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: TDD prompts and test-first patterns from the Claude Code team.

modules.title: Iterative refinement loops
modules.why: Specs are rarely complete first try. The agent runs, fails a test, you adjust the spec, repeat. Treat the spec as the source of truth, not the code.
modules.sf_bridge: Like sprint refinement — the doc and the build evolve together. The doc wins ties.

resources.title: Simon Willison weblog
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: The best running journal of how iterative AI coding actually plays out.

resources.title: Spec-first AI development
resources.author: Sean Goedecke
resources.summary: Reread after a few weeks of practice — different parts hit.

modules.title: The open-weights revolution
modules.why: Llama 4.1, DeepSeek V3.1, Qwen 3.6, Gemma 4.2, Mistral Large 3 closed most of the gap with the closed frontier in 2026. You can now run a serious model on your own hardware or a rented GPU.
modules.sf_bridge: Like the day OSS Postgres + a VPS started competing with managed clouds — vendor leverage shifts. The architect's job becomes 'build vs buy', and 'build' is finally viable for AI.

resources.title: Open LLM Leaderboard
resources.author: Hugging Face
resources.summary: The standard scoreboard. Skim the top 20 every quarter.

resources.title: DeepSeek
resources.author: DeepSeek AI
resources.summary: The current open-weights price/performance leader.

resources.title: Qwen
resources.author: Alibaba
resources.summary: Strong all-round family with excellent code-tuned variants.

modules.title: Reasoning models in depth
modules.why: o3, GPT-5-thinking, DeepSeek-R1, Claude with extended thinking — different price/latency profile, much better on hard problems. Know when to reach for one.
modules.sf_bridge: Like batch Apex vs synchronous Apex — different runtime, different cost shape, used for different problems. You don't run reasoning models on every request.

resources.title: Introducing o3 and o4-mini
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: The reasoning lineup as of 2025.

resources.title: DeepSeek-R1 paper
resources.author: DeepSeek
resources.summary: How an open model matched o1 with reinforcement learning. Skim section 2.

resources.title: Extended thinking
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Practical use of Claude's reasoning mode in code.

modules.title: DeepSeek — the open-source benchmark
modules.why: DeepSeek V3 and R1 redefined what open weights can do. Required reading to understand the open frontier and why prices crashed.
modules.sf_bridge: Like an unmanaged package that out-performs the AppExchange paid alternative — and is free. Eats vendor margin everywhere.

resources.title: DeepSeek-V3 technical report
resources.author: DeepSeek
resources.summary: How they trained a frontier model on a fraction of the budget. Section 1 + 4.

resources.title: DeepSeek-R1 paper
resources.author: DeepSeek
resources.summary: The reasoning model. RL recipe + distillation.

resources.title: DeepSeek on HuggingFace
resources.author: DeepSeek
resources.summary: All weights, configs, and quantizations. Pull and run.

modules.title: Qwen, Llama, Gemma, Mistral, Kimi — picking an open model
modules.why: Beyond DeepSeek, several open families compete: Llama 4.1 (Meta), Qwen 3.6 (Alibaba), Gemma 4.2 (Google), Mistral Large 3, Kimi K2 (Moonshot). Pick by use case — coding, long-context, small/efficient, reasoning.
modules.sf_bridge: AppExchange shopping — you don't install all of them. Pick the one that fits your job-to-be-done.

resources.title: Qwen 3.6 release
resources.author: Alibaba
resources.summary: Best open generalist with open coding variants.

resources.title: Llama models
resources.author: Meta
resources.summary: Llama 4 family. Strong fine-tune ecosystem.

resources.title: Gemma 4.2
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Google's open small-model family. Mobile-friendly sizes from 1B.

resources.title: Mistral models
resources.author: Mistral AI
resources.summary: Mistral Large 3 + Codestral. European, friendlier licensing.

modules.title: Small language models & on-device
modules.why: Phi-4, Gemma 4, Llama 3.2 1-3B, Apple Intelligence Foundation models run on a laptop or phone. For privacy-sensitive code, they're the only option.
modules.sf_bridge: Like running Apex in a private sandbox vs sending it through a managed package — control vs capability trade-off. Smaller models, but yours.

resources.title: Phi-4
resources.author: Microsoft
resources.summary: 14B model that punches above its weight. Best small reasoning.

resources.title: Gemma 4.2
resources.author: Google
resources.summary: Google's small-model family. 1B-27B sizes.

resources.title: Apple Foundation Models
resources.author: Apple
resources.summary: On-device models designed for personal context. Good architectural read.

modules.title: Memory architectures
modules.why: Models are stateless. Memory is built on top: short-term (in-context), long-term (vector DB / files), procedural (skills / system prompts). Each layer has trade-offs.
modules.sf_bridge: Memory layers ≈ session vars + Custom Settings + Custom Metadata + indexed Files. Different lifetimes, different costs, picked per use case.

resources.title: MemGPT / Letta
resources.author: Letta
resources.summary: The 'OS for memory' framing. Useful even if you don't use the lib.

resources.title: Agent skills
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How skills + project memory work in Claude's stack.

modules.title: Long-running & background agents
modules.why: Agents that run for minutes-to-hours need different infrastructure than chat: resumable, pausable, observable. This is where 'agent' becomes 'service'.
modules.sf_bridge: Like batch Apex / Platform Events — same logic, but built for the long haul with checkpoints and visibility.

resources.title: Headless Claude Code
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to run agents in CI / cron / background services.

resources.title: Cognition blog
resources.author: Cognition
resources.summary: Devin team's notes on long-running agents in production.

modules.title: Multi-agent systems
modules.why: Multiple specialized agents collaborating (researcher, coder, reviewer). Better on complex tasks, harder to debug. Frameworks: AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph.
modules.sf_bridge: Like multiple Permission Sets on the same User — composability with coordination cost. Power per role, friction at the seams.

resources.title: AutoGen
resources.author: Microsoft
resources.summary: The reference multi-agent framework.

resources.title: CrewAI
resources.author: CrewAI
resources.summary: Higher-level, role-based framing.

resources.title: LangGraph
resources.author: LangChain
resources.summary: Graph-based orchestration. Production-friendly.

modules.title: Advanced retrieval — rerankers, hybrid search
modules.why: Naive vector search retrieves irrelevant chunks. Hybrid (keyword + vector) and rerankers (Cohere, Voyage) close the gap.
modules.sf_bridge: Like adding SOSL + SOQL with a relevance sort — semantic recall + lexical precision in one pipeline.

resources.title: Cohere Rerank
resources.author: Cohere
resources.summary: Best-in-class reranker. Drop it in front of any retrieval system.

resources.title: Voyage AI
resources.author: Voyage
resources.summary: Voyage's embeddings + rerank. Often best-of-class on coding queries.

resources.title: Context engineering
resources.author: Lance Martin
resources.summary: The retrieval-stack architecture, end-to-end.

modules.title: Code interpreters & sandboxes
modules.why: Letting an agent execute code is the most useful single tool. Sandboxing it safely is non-trivial.
modules.sf_bridge: Like running anonymous Apex in a sandbox — powerful, requires guardrails. Prod-grade agents need a containerized blast-radius.

resources.title: E2B sandboxes
resources.author: E2B
resources.summary: The standard agent sandbox. Open source, drop-in.

resources.title: Code execution tool
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Anthropic's hosted sandbox tool. Zero-config option.

resources.title: Code Interpreter
resources.author: OpenAI
resources.summary: OpenAI's hosted equivalent. Worth knowing the differences.

modules.title: Devin & autonomous agents
modules.why: Cognition's Devin is the headline 'fully autonomous' coding agent. Useful to understand the ceiling of current autonomy and where the costs and failures show up.
modules.sf_bridge: Like an outsourced contractor — you describe the ticket, they ship a PR. Good for well-scoped work, oversight still required, billing per task.

resources.title: Devin
resources.author: Cognition
resources.summary: Product. Pricing + when it's worth using.

resources.title: Cognition blog
resources.author: Cognition
resources.summary: Engineering posts on building long-running agents.

modules.title: Agent observability & tracing
modules.why: When an agent fails after 30 tool calls, you need a trace. LangSmith, Braintrust, Helicone exist for exactly this.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Debug Logs + Apex Replay Debugger — but for probabilistic, multi-step decision trees. Same instinct, more dimensions.

resources.title: LangSmith
resources.author: LangChain
resources.summary: The most-used trace + eval platform.

resources.title: Braintrust
resources.author: Braintrust
resources.summary: Strong on evals + production tracing combined.

resources.title: GenAI semantic conventions
resources.author: OpenTelemetry
resources.summary: Vendor-neutral tracing spec. Good to know what fields exist.

modules.title: Evals — eval-driven development
modules.why: Once you build something with an agent, how do you know it works — not just today, but after the model updates next month? This is where enterprises hit a wall.
modules.sf_bridge: Evals ≈ Apex test classes + regression suite, but for probabilistic output. You check behavior across a test set, not a single assertion.

resources.title: Your AI product needs evals
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.summary: The definitive post on eval-driven development. Practical, not academic.

resources.title: Field guide
resources.author: Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar
resources.summary: How to move from prototype to production. What to measure, what to ignore.

modules.title: Building eval datasets
modules.why: Real evals come from real production traces, labeled by humans. Synthetic-only datasets miss what real users break.
modules.sf_bridge: Like building a sandbox dataset from production for UAT — same instinct, more iteration. The dataset is the asset, not the model.

resources.title: Evals FAQ
resources.author: Hamel Husain
resources.summary: Practical Q&A. What to do when you have no data yet.

resources.title: Develop test cases
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Anthropic's structured approach to building eval sets.

modules.title: LLM-as-judge
modules.why: For open-ended outputs (summaries, code), use a model to score outputs against criteria. Cheap, scalable, has known biases — calibrate carefully.
modules.sf_bridge: Like a Validation Rule that's itself a formula calling a service — flexible but watch the second-order failure modes.

resources.title: Eval tool (Claude)
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Claude's first-party eval workflow. Includes judge templates.

resources.title: LLM evaluators
resources.author: LangSmith
resources.summary: Concrete patterns for grading model output with a model.

modules.title: Observability & tracing in production
modules.why: Logs aren't enough. You need step-level traces with token counts, costs, tool calls, and decisions — across thousands of agent runs.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Setup → Debug Logs but per decision step, with cost columns and a search index. Not optional once you're in prod.

resources.title: LangSmith
resources.author: LangChain
resources.summary: The most-used trace + eval platform.

resources.title: Langfuse
resources.author: Langfuse
resources.summary: Open-source alternative. Self-host friendly.

resources.title: Helicone
resources.author: Helicone
resources.summary: Proxy-based observability. Drop-in for OpenAI-compatible APIs.

modules.title: Security — prompt injection
modules.why: Prompt injection is the #1 systemic risk. Untrusted text reaching the model can override its instructions. There is no full fix yet.
modules.sf_bridge: Like SQL injection in 2005 — the new attack class everyone underestimates. Same lesson: never trust string concatenation across trust boundaries.

resources.title: Prompt injection
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: The running archive. Start with the most recent posts.

resources.title: OWASP LLM Top 10
resources.author: OWASP
resources.summary: The standard threat list. Map it to your system.

resources.title: Mitigate jailbreaks
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: Concrete defensive techniques. None are silver bullets.

modules.title: Security — secrets, sandboxing & supply chain
modules.why: An agent with shell access can leak credentials, run arbitrary npm installs, exfiltrate code. Constrain the blast radius.
modules.sf_bridge: Like setting Apex sharing rules + Permission Sets — least privilege all the way down. The agent gets exactly what it needs, nothing more.

resources.title: Claude Code permissions
resources.author: Anthropic
resources.summary: How to lock down what Claude Code can read, write, run.

resources.title: The lethal trifecta
resources.author: Simon Willison
resources.summary: Private data + untrusted content + external comms = exfiltration risk. Keep them separate.

modules.title: Cost monitoring at scale
modules.why: An agent running unsupervised can burn $1k/day. You want hard caps, not just dashboards.
modules.sf_bridge: Like Limits class checks in Apex — fail fast before you hit the wall. Cost is a runtime constraint, not a billing afterthought.

resources.title: Helicone
resources.author: Helicone
resources.summary: Proxy-based cost tracking + rate limits.

resources.title: Langfuse cost tracking
resources.author: Langfuse
resources.summary: Per-run cost rollups, with model-aware accounting.

modules.title: Deploying agents to production
modules.why: Pulling it all together — pick model, write spec, build evals, ship behind a feature flag, monitor, iterate. The architect's loop, applied to AI.
modules.sf_bridge: Like a release management plan — sandbox, UAT, prod, with rollback. Same gates, different runtime.

resources.title: Field guide
resources.author: Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar
resources.summary: The end-to-end playbook for shipping LLM products.

resources.title: Patterns for building LLM systems
resources.author: Eugene Yan
resources.summary: Architectural patterns. The 'design book' of the AI stack.

use_cases.title: Talk to your Salesforce org
use_cases.scenario: Ask your org questions in plain English. 'How many open opportunities over 100k closing this quarter, by region?'
use_cases.outcome: A local MCP server exposing your org to any agent. Connect it to Claude Code or Cursor and your data becomes a typed tool the model can call — no SOQL by hand.

use_cases.title: Apex PR reviewer
use_cases.scenario: Every Apex PR gets an automatic review for governor-limit risk, security, naming conventions, and missing test coverage.
use_cases.outcome: Headless Claude Code triggered by GitHub Actions on each PR. Posts inline comments. Saves 30–60 minutes per PR.

use_cases.title: Spec → Apex test class
use_cases.scenario: Paste a user story with acceptance criteria. Get a working Apex test class with positive, negative, and bulk cases that compiles.
use_cases.outcome: A small CLI prompt that turns markdown specs into test classes covering all branches. Cuts the boring part of TDD by 80%.

use_cases.title: Design doc → org metadata
use_cases.scenario: Write a feature design doc. Agent produces SObject XML, permission set, validation rules, and a Flow scaffold — reviewable diff before deploy.
use_cases.outcome: A repo with a 'specs/' folder. Each spec is consumed by Claude Code; output goes to 'force-app/'. Architect-grade output, junior-dev hours.

use_cases.title: Debug log triage
use_cases.scenario: Paste a 5MB debug log. Get a 5-line root cause + 3 candidate fixes ranked by likelihood.
use_cases.outcome: A simple prompt template + one-file CLI. Tackles the 'silent Flow failure' and 'governor limit blown deep in subroutine' patterns.

use_cases.title: Org health daily digest
use_cases.scenario: Every morning at 7am, your phone buzzes with one paragraph: failed Flows overnight, integration retries, governor-limit close calls.
use_cases.outcome: Headless agent on a cron schedule. Pulls Setup logs via API, summarizes, posts to Slack/Teams.

use_cases.title: Migration script writer
use_cases.scenario: Feed legacy schema (CSV / DDL). Get migration Apex, ETL plan, rollback Apex, and a smoke-test script.
use_cases.outcome: A repeatable process for the painful first sprint of every Salesforce migration. Removes 60% of the boilerplate work.

use_cases.title: Customer call brief
use_cases.scenario: Before a prospect call, agent reads their website + your Account history and produces a 1-pager — pain points, objections, the right opener.
use_cases.outcome: One-button workflow that turns 30 minutes of pre-call research into 30 seconds. Compounds across a full sales pipeline.

use_cases.title: Why is this Flow failing?
use_cases.scenario: Paste a Flow XML + recent debug log. Get a hypothesis + the specific node to inspect + a proposed fix.
use_cases.outcome: A targeted diagnostician for the silent-Flow-failure class of bug. Skips the 'I'll come back to this tomorrow' frustration.

use_cases.title: Onboarding doc agent
use_cases.scenario: Point at a repo (Apex + Flows + LWC). Get a CLAUDE.md, an architect briefing, a glossary of org-specific terms, and a 'getting started' checklist.
use_cases.outcome: Onboarding for new hires drops from 2 weeks to 2 days. Re-runs auto-update the doc as the org changes.

stack.name: Claude Sonnet 4.7
stack.oneliner: Default daily-driver for coding in 2026. Best long-context reasoning at this price tier.
stack.sf: The 'all-around enterprise developer' model — picks up nuance, holds discipline.

stack.name: Claude Opus 4.7
stack.oneliner: Top-tier reasoning. Use for hard architectural questions; expensive per token.
stack.sf: The principal architect you call in for the hardest design reviews.

stack.name: GPT-5
stack.oneliner: OpenAI's frontier general model. Strong on tool use, code, and structured output.
stack.sf: Salesforce-branded all-in-one — strong defaults across the surface.

stack.name: GPT-5-Codex
stack.oneliner: Code-tuned GPT-5 powering OpenAI Codex CLI/cloud. Best inside the OpenAI agent stack.
stack.sf: The vertical edition tuned for one job — coding.

stack.name: o3 / o4-mini
stack.oneliner: OpenAI's reasoning models. Slow + expensive, but unmatched on multi-step problems.
stack.sf: Async batch Apex of LLMs — different runtime, harder problems.

stack.name: Gemini 3 Pro
stack.oneliner: Long-context champion (1M+ tokens). Strong multimodal — feeds whole repos and videos.
stack.sf: When the context window is your bottleneck, this is the escape hatch.

stack.name: Gemini 3 Flash
stack.oneliner: The cheap-and-fast workhorse. Great for high-volume agent loops where Sonnet is overkill.
stack.sf: The Lightning Component of LLMs — small, fast, deployed everywhere.

stack.name: Grok 3
stack.oneliner: xAI's frontier model. Strong reasoning and uncensored long-form output. Real-time X data integration.
stack.sf: The challenger vendor — sometimes worth a side-by-side test on niche tasks.

stack.name: DeepSeek V3.1
stack.oneliner: Flagship open-weights generalist. Closes most of the gap with frontier closed models, at a fraction of cost.
stack.sf: The open-source unmanaged package outperforming the paid AppExchange option.

stack.name: DeepSeek-R1
stack.oneliner: First open reasoning model to match o1-class performance. Distillations run on consumer GPUs.
stack.sf: The open answer to commercial reasoning models. Self-host friendly.

stack.name: Qwen 3.6 Coder
stack.oneliner: The state-of-the-art open coding model for 2026. Unmatched performance on repo-scale reasoning.
stack.sf: The gold-standard open vertical for coding — outperforms many closed models.

stack.name: Gemma 4.2
stack.oneliner: Google's flagship open family. Highly optimized for on-device and edge inference with native multimodal support.
stack.sf: Google's on-device masterpiece — fast, private, and exceptionally smart.

stack.name: Llama 4.1
stack.oneliner: The most widely adopted open frontier family. New 4.1 revision improves agentic tool-use consistency.
stack.sf: The open-source 'Linux' of AI — the default choice for most enterprise builders.

stack.name: gpt-oss-120b
stack.oneliner: OpenAI's first true open-weights release. 120B and 20B sizes; strong general capability with permissive license.
stack.sf: The pivot moment — when even OpenAI shipped open weights, vendor lock-in stopped being inevitable.

stack.name: Mistral Large 3
stack.oneliner: European open option with strong coding (via Codestral) and serious enterprise support.
stack.sf: The European Salesforce — sovereignty-friendly, enterprise-ready.

stack.name: Codestral 3
stack.oneliner: Mistral's coding model, refreshed. Tuned for inline completion latency and code edits.
stack.sf: European answer to Qwen Coder. Strong on European-language codebases too.

stack.name: Kimi K2
stack.oneliner: Long-context champion among open models. Useful when you can't (or won't) pay Gemini 3 prices.
stack.sf: Open answer for repo-scale context windows.

stack.name: Grok-OSS
stack.oneliner: xAI's open-weights releases (Grok-1, Grok-2). Useful baseline for fine-tuning experiments.
stack.sf: Vendor-released weights — more about transparency than performance leadership.

stack.name: Phi-4
stack.oneliner: 14B model that punches above its weight. Best small model for reasoning on a laptop GPU.
stack.sf: The Lightning Out edition — runs anywhere, surprisingly capable.

stack.name: Claude Code
stack.oneliner: Terminal-native coding agent. Strong for spec-driven work, deep codebases, headless CI runs.
stack.sf: SFDX CLI of agents — terminal-first, scriptable, composable.

stack.name: OpenAI Codex CLI
stack.oneliner: OpenAI's relaunched coding agent. CLI client is open source; backed by GPT-5-Codex.
stack.sf: OpenAI counterpart to Claude Code. Pick by which model drives the loop.

stack.name: Aider
stack.oneliner: Model-agnostic open-source CLI agent. Git-integrated, scriptable, your fallback when commercial tools are blocked.
stack.sf: Open-source SFDX plugin — community-driven, swap engines freely.

stack.name: Continue
stack.oneliner: Open-source autopilot for IDEs (VS Code, JetBrains). Bring your own model.
stack.sf: The open IDE plugin layer — works across editors.

stack.name: Cline
stack.oneliner: VS Code agent with strong tool-use. Acts on your filesystem with explicit approval per step.
stack.sf: Like inline-approval workflows — every action is gated until you sign off.

stack.name: Cursor
stack.oneliner: VS Code fork with a built-in agent (Composer). The most-used agentic IDE in 2026.
stack.sf: VS Code with the AI extensions, but tighter integration. Lowest switching cost from existing IDE habits.

stack.name: Google Antigravity
stack.oneliner: Agent-first IDE launched Nov 2025. Agents run in browser, terminal, and editor concurrently. Multi-model.
stack.sf: Brand-new IDE built around agents from day one — not retrofitted onto VS Code.

stack.name: Windsurf
stack.oneliner: Codeium's agentic IDE (now Cognition-owned). Cascade flow excels at multi-file edits.
stack.sf: Another VS Code fork — pick by which agent loop fits your brain.

stack.name: WebStorm + Junie
stack.oneliner: JetBrains' AI agent (Junie) embedded across IntelliJ family. Lowest-friction option for JetBrains shops.
stack.sf: Your existing IDE, plus an agent — refactors and shortcuts you already know stay intact.

stack.name: Zed
stack.oneliner: Native, fast editor with first-class AI features. Built in Rust; pairs natively with multiple models.
stack.sf: The 'high-performance' editor — for when VS Code's latency annoys you.

stack.name: GitHub Copilot Workspace
stack.oneliner: Issue-to-PR workflow built into GitHub. Strong for teams already using GitHub Issues / Projects.
stack.sf: Where work lives in your tracker, not your editor — agent runs there.

stack.name: Devin
stack.oneliner: The headline autonomous coding agent. Picks up tickets, ships PRs, reports back.
stack.sf: Outsourced contractor model — task in, PR out, billed per task.

stack.name: Replit Agent
stack.oneliner: Build full apps from a prompt; runs in Replit's hosted environment.
stack.sf: Quick-build Lightning App from a description — deployable instantly.

stack.name: v0
stack.oneliner: UI / app generator from prompts. Strongest for React + shadcn-style frontends.
stack.sf: UI Builder for arbitrary React apps — design-to-code in one step.

stack.name: Bolt.new
stack.oneliner: In-browser full-stack app generator with WebContainers. Works without local install.
stack.sf: Sandbox-in-a-browser — provision and ship a full app without an env.

stack.name: Lovable
stack.oneliner: Prompt → production web app. Strong on full-stack scaffolds with auth + DB included.
stack.sf: Closer to a managed package builder — opinionated stack, fast to value.

glossary.definition: The total text (prompt + history + tool results) the model can 'see' in one call. Measured in tokens (~0.75 words). Bigger = more expensive and slower.
glossary.sf: Governor limit for a single transaction.

glossary.definition: The unit of text the model processes. 1 token ≈ 4 characters of English. You pay per token in and out.
glossary.sf: API call count — metered and priced per unit.

glossary.definition: Instructions given to the model before the user says anything. Sets role, rules, tone.
glossary.sf: Org-wide default settings applied to every session.

glossary.definition: The model outputs a structured request to run a named function with typed args. The runtime executes it and feeds the result back.
glossary.sf: Invocable Apex action called from a Flow.

glossary.definition: Open protocol for exposing tools and data sources to any agent, regardless of model vendor.
glossary.sf: Named Credentials + External Services — a standard way to plug an external capability into any consumer.

glossary.definition: A loop that runs the model, lets it call tools, feeds results back, and repeats until a goal is reached.
glossary.sf: A Flow that can loop and call Apex actions, where the decision logic is the LLM instead of declarative rules.

glossary.definition: A separate agent invocation spawned by a parent agent to handle a subtask with its own fresh context window.
glossary.sf: Queueable chain — offload work to keep the parent transaction lean.

glossary.definition: Reusing the same system prompt / file contents across calls so you're only charged (and delayed by) the new part.
glossary.sf: Platform cache — store the expensive-to-compute prefix once, reuse it.

glossary.definition: Summarizing old conversation turns to fit under the context limit when a session runs long.
glossary.sf: Archiving old records to keep a live table performant.

glossary.definition: Write a precise specification first; the agent generates implementation + tests + iterates until the spec is satisfied.
glossary.sf: Solution design document → build handoff, but the agent is the dev.

glossary.definition: A test suite for probabilistic output. Run the same prompt across many cases and measure a quality metric.
glossary.sf: Apex test class + regression pack for AI output.

glossary.definition: Fetch relevant documents at query time and include them in the prompt, so the model answers from your data instead of memory.
glossary.sf: SOQL + formula — pull the right records, then reason over them.

glossary.definition: A vector representation of a chunk of text. Used to find semantically similar content (the 'retrieval' in RAG).
glossary.sf: A hashed/indexed field for fuzzy lookup — but the similarity is semantic, not literal.

glossary.definition: When a model produces confident-sounding output that is factually wrong. Reduced (not eliminated) by retrieval, tool use, and evals.
glossary.sf: A Flow path that runs silently with the wrong filter — looks fine, gives the wrong answer.

glossary.definition: A model trained to 'think' before answering — emits hidden reasoning tokens, then a final answer. Slower, pricier, much better on hard problems.
glossary.sf: Async batch Apex of LLMs — different runtime profile, picked for harder problems.

glossary.definition: A model whose trained parameters are publicly downloadable. Doesn't always mean open license — read the terms.
glossary.sf: Like an unmanaged package — source visible, but license rules still apply.

glossary.definition: Reason → Act → Observe loop. The agent decides, runs a tool, observes the result, decides again. The pattern under most coding agents.
glossary.sf: Decision element → Action element → loop, with each result feeding back into the next decision.

glossary.definition: Using a model to score outputs against criteria. Cheap eval automation, with calibration and bias caveats.
glossary.sf: A Validation Rule that's itself a service call — flexible but watch the second-order failure modes.

glossary.definition: Untrusted text reaching the model overrides its instructions. The #1 systemic security risk for agent systems.
glossary.sf: SQL injection, but for prompts. Don't concatenate untrusted strings across trust boundaries.

glossary.definition: Reusable, packaged instructions + assets that an agent loads on demand. Way to give an agent durable, scoped capabilities.
glossary.sf: Like an installed package — adds named capabilities the agent can invoke when relevant.

glossary.definition: A second-pass model that re-scores retrieved chunks for relevance. Improves RAG quality dramatically with little code.
glossary.sf: Like adding a relevance ORDER BY after your SOQL — same recall, much better precision.

glossary.definition: An isolated runtime where an agent can execute code without touching your host. Limits blast radius.
glossary.sf: Salesforce sandbox, but for arbitrary code execution. Required for prod-grade agents.

glossary.definition: An agent that runs without a UI — invoked from CI, cron, or another service. Same loop, no chat surface.
glossary.sf: Like Apex run from CI/Bash — no UI, all programmatic, fits into pipelines.

glossary.definition: Per-token price for input + output. Reasoning models also bill 'thinking' tokens you don't see.
glossary.sf: Metered API usage with cost per row — same instinct, different units.

glossary.definition: A vendor-published document on a model's capabilities, limits, and risks. Useful for due diligence and model selection.
glossary.sf: Like release notes + impact assessment combined — read before adopting.

glossary.definition: A database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors (embeddings).
glossary.sf: Data Cloud for semantic data — finding records by meaning, not just ID.

glossary.definition: Searching by meaning rather than exact keyword matches.
glossary.sf: Global Search but it understands what you meant, not just what you typed.

glossary.definition: A mathematical measure of how similar two vectors (and thus two pieces of text) are.
glossary.sf: The 'MATCH' score in a fuzzy lookup or deduplication rule.

glossary.definition: A technique for fine-tuning a model on specific data using very few parameters.
glossary.sf: A 'Skinny' version of a full metadata deploy — fast, targeted, and lightweight.

glossary.definition: Adjusting a pre-trained model on a specific dataset to improve performance on a task.
glossary.sf: Customizing a Managed Package for your specific Org's business logic.

glossary.definition: Compressing a model by reducing the precision of its weights (e.g., 16-bit to 4-bit).
glossary.sf: Archiving old data to save storage while keeping core records accessible.

glossary.definition: Using human feedback to align a model's behavior with human preferences.
glossary.sf: A UAT feedback loop that permanently improves the underlying automation logic.

glossary.definition: A model that can process multiple types of input (text, images, audio, video).
glossary.sf: A Record where Attachments, Notes, and Voice Memos are all natively indexed together.

glossary.definition: A sampling technique that picks from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p.
glossary.sf: Filtering a picklist to only the most likely valid options for a given context.

glossary.definition: A specific string that tells the model to stop generating further text.
glossary.sf: The 'break' statement in an Apex loop or the 'End' node in a Flow.

glossary.definition: Forcing the model to output valid JSON for easier programmatic consumption.
glossary.sf: Setting an API return type to 'Application/JSON' and enforcing a strict schema.

glossary.definition: Prompting the model to 'think out loud' step-by-step before providing a final answer.
glossary.sf: Adding detailed comments and intermediate variables in a complex Apex method.

glossary.definition: Providing several examples in the prompt to show the model exactly how to respond.
glossary.sf: Writing a unit test with multiple setup cases to define expected behavior.

glossary.definition: Asking the model to perform a task without providing any prior examples.
glossary.sf: Handing a user story to a developer who has never seen the codebase before.

glossary.definition: Training a smaller, faster model to mimic the behavior of a larger, more capable one.
glossary.sf: Refactoring a complex, multi-step Flow into a single, efficient Apex trigger.

glossary.definition: Managing the flow of data and control between multiple models, tools, and systems.
glossary.sf: An OmniStudio Integration Procedure or a complex Flow Orchestrator.

glossary.definition: Ensuring model responses are based strictly on provided facts or retrieved data.
glossary.sf: Enforcing that a Flow only uses fields from the current record and its parents.

glossary.definition: An attempt to bypass a model's safety guardrails via clever or adversarial prompting.
glossary.sf: An exploit that bypasses 'With Sharing' rules or Validation Rules via a system-mode hole.

glossary.definition: Adversarial testing where humans try to find weaknesses, biases, and safety issues in a model.
glossary.sf: Penetration testing your Org's security model and sharing rules.

glossary.definition: The process of ensuring an AI's goals and behaviors match human values and intentions.
glossary.sf: Governance and Compliance oversight for your automated business systems.

glossary.definition: A secret token used to authenticate and authorize requests to an AI model provider.
glossary.sf: The Client Secret for an OAuth Connected App or a Named Credential token.

glossary.definition: Hard restrictions on the number of requests or tokens processed in a specific time period.
glossary.sf: Concurrent Request Limits, Hourly Sync Limits, or Daily API Call Quotas.

glossary.definition: The algorithm that breaks raw text into tokens for the model to process.
glossary.sf: The parser that turns your source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) for the compiler.

glossary.definition: The total time elapsed between sending a prompt and receiving the full response.
glossary.sf: Page load time or the time taken for a Trigger to complete a save operation.

glossary.definition: The total volume of data (tokens) a model can process or generate in a set time.
glossary.sf: Batch processing speed — how many records your system can handle per hour.

glossary.definition: The latency threshold that 99% of all requests fall below.
glossary.sf: The 'worst-case' performance experienced by your users during peak org activity.
